annotations.text: I shall therefore focus on neurogenic tumors of the posterior mediastinum as the primary neoplastic consideration in this infant.

For a diagnosis in this case I would have obtained an assay for urinary catecholamine values and additional imaging to delineate the vascular features, and I would have proceeded with an exploratory thoracotomy.
I propose that a thoracotomy was performed and that an extralobar sequestration was detected.
I wondered whether the infant might have had hypertension caused by a functioning neuroendocrine tumor.
If there had been air it would have been easy to detect it on the CT scan.
The limit of resolution is approximately 5 mm.
The combination of diagnostic information, prevention of future infections, prevention of spontaneous bleeding, and allowance for unimpeded lung growth produces a compelling argument for removal.

the lack of reported polyhydramnios during gestation, the fact that the patient was a full-term infant, and the slight and rapidly resolving respiratory distress lead me away from the diagnosis of a congenital cystic adenomatoid malformation.
The presentation of a benign or a malignant neoplasm at birth is unusual.
I agree with them, although I would have needed the added comfort provided by a negative assay of urine for catecholamines.
About 90 percent of patients with thoracic neural-crest tumors have elevated levels of dopa, dopamine, norepinephrine, normetanephrine, vanilmandelic acid (VMA), and homovanillic acid (HVA).
Approximately 50 percent of patients with extralobar sequestration have a variety of other congenital anomalies, particularly diaphragmatic defects, but none have been demonstrated in this patient.
The disorganized architecture in this case is typical of extralobar sequestrations and resembles that seen in one type of cystic adenomatoid malformation.
Lymphangiectasia is seen regularly in extrapulmonary sequestration and may simulate congenital cystic lymphangiectasis.
